rules.message: Network call made structurally inside a for/for-of/for-in/while/do-while statement or an Array forEach/map/filter/reduce callback — verified against the parser's projected loop spans (the call's line falls within a loop statement's span, or within an array-iteration callback's own span), not merely co-occurring with loop syntax somewhere in the same function — so it fires one request per iteration, the HTTP analogue of N+1 (rate-limit / serial-latency risk). Batch via a bulk endpoint, or gather inputs and issue one request (or a bounded Promise.all). A file with no projected loop spans is silently skipped by this rule — for the `.ts`/`.tsx` files this rule scans, that realistically means a degraded parse (oversized file or syntax failure), the same graceful-degrade policy method-scan already applies to missing symbol spans. Suppress a vetted case with `// api-in-loop-ok`.

TypeORM's `eager: true` relation option (typically on `@OneToMany`/`@ManyToOne`/`@OneToOne`/`@ManyToMany`/`@JoinTable` option objects — the matcher fires on any `eager: true` line in a file with TypeORM/Sequelize signals, a deliberate heuristic), or Sequelize's `include: [{ all: true }]` — an entity-level eager-load declaration that makes EVERY finder/query of this entity join the association, whether or not that particular caller needs it: the query-time opposite of N+1, over-fetching on every read instead of under-fetching per iteration. Default relations to lazy and opt in per query instead — TypeORM's `relations: [...]`/`relations: { ... }` on the specific `find`/`findOne` call, or Sequelize's own `include: [...]` at the call site — so only the queries that actually use the association pay for the join. Suppress a vetted case (e.g. a small lookup table genuinely read together on every access) with `// eager-relation-ok`.

An explicit `fetch = FetchType.EAGER` on a JPA/Hibernate mapping annotation (`@OneToMany`/`@ManyToOne`/`@OneToOne`/`@ManyToMany`/`@ElementCollection` — the matcher fires on the `fetch = FetchType.EAGER` attribute itself, whichever annotation carries it) forces every finder/query of the owning entity to join and load that association, whether or not the caller needs it — most expensive on `@OneToMany`/`@ManyToMany` collections, where it can pull an unbounded child set on every parent read. Prefer `FetchType.LAZY` and fetch the association explicitly per query that needs it (a JPQL `JOIN FETCH`, an entity graph, or a dedicated projection). Disclosure: this rule only catches the EXPLICIT declaration above — `@ManyToOne`/`@OneToOne` default to EAGER in the JPA spec with no `fetch` attribute at all, so a clean scan here is not proof the entity has no eager-loading cost. Suppress a vetted case with `// jpa-eager-ok`.
